input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-05T00:46:57.535Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) and is hitting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference — MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated that as success.

Let me look at their project files to give a precise fix.

data.content:
This is a known MTP behavioral difference. **Exit code 8** means "no tests discovered" — MTP treats this as an error, while VSTest silently returned 0.

Since all your test code is behind `#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS`, when that symbol isn't defined, the assembly has zero tests and MTP exits with code 8.

**Fix:** Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` so MTP returns 0 when no tests are found. You can bake this into the project so CI doesn't need changes:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered (e.g. all tests compiled out).
         VSTest returned 0 in this case. Ignore exit code 8 to restore that behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. With MTP, exit code 8 means "no tests were discovered." When `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` is not defined, all test code is compiled out, resulting in zero tests, which MTP treats as an error (exit code 8).

The fix is to set the `<TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>` property... wait, no. The correct property is to configure what happens when no tests are found.

Actually, the right MSBuild property for this is:

```xml
<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>


```

But let me reconsider the exact property name. I could also use a command-line flag like `--ignore-exit-code 8`, or there's an MSBuild property that prevents the process from exiting with an error when no tests are found—something like `<TestingPlatformExitProcessOnNoTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnNoTests>`.

Actually, the cleanest approach is probably just passing `--ignore-exit-code 8` to `dotnet test`, which tells it to treat the "no tests found" exit code as success. There might be an MSBuild property for this too, but I'm not entirely certain of the exact name.

```
dotnet test -- --ignore-exit-code 8
```

Or alternatively, `--minimum-expected-tests 0` would signal to MTP that having zero tests is acceptable. Let me think about which one to recommend for the project-level fix. I'm settling on the clearest solution: exit code 8 means no tests were discovered in MTP, whereas VSTest returned 0 in that case, so the fix is to pass `--ignore-exit-code 8` to the test command to normalize the behavior. I'm weighing between `--ignore-exit-code 8` and `--minimum-expected-tests 0` as the fix—the latter feels more semantically correct since it explicitly allows zero tests, while the former just suppresses the error code. I'll go with `--minimum-expected-tests 0` as it's the cleaner approach.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered (e.g. all tests compiled out).
         VSTest returned 0 in this case. Ignore exit code 8 to restore that behavior. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content: Both scenarios work. Here's the final project file:

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
2.   <PropertyGroup>
3.     <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
4.     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
5.     <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
6.     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
7.     <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered (e.g. all tests compiled out).
8.          VSTest returned 0 in this case. Ignore exit code 8 to restore that behavior. -->
9.     <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
10.   </PropertyGroup>
11.   <ItemGroup>
12.     <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="3.8.0" />
13.   </ItemGroup>
14. </Project>
15.

   </ItemGroup>

data.content:
**Summary:**

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Zero tests (no `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS`) | ❌ Exit code 8 | ✅ Exit code 0 |
| Tests present | ✅ Exit code 0 | ✅ Exit code 0 |

The one-line fix is `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` in the `<PropertyGroup>`. This tells MTP to return 0 instead of 8 when no tests are discovered, restoring the VSTest behavior. If you have multiple test projects, consider putting this in a `Directory.Build.props` instead.
